decomposition.title: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing, enterprise IT infrastructure, cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting, digital innovation
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget stated. Inferred potential future hosting/transformation opportunity: approximately $15M-$50M over 3-5 years based on enterprise-scale Wisconsin data center exit, large VMware estate, SAP/non-SAP hosting, DR, networking, landing zone, migration, and possible managed services. Phase 0 assessment-only work is likely much smaller, approximately $250k-$1M.
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated. Inferred commercial modeling period: likely 3-5 years for future hosting/DCaaS/hybrid services, with this RFP itself limited to Phase 0 evaluation and later phases to define final contract terms.

requirements.text: Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Submission timing/channel is easy to treat as administrative, but late or misdirected proposals are commonly rejected before evaluation.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response Template provided as part of this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The required response template is referenced separately from the main scope and may be missed if the Excel/template package is not controlled.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: A superficially compliant template with Yes/No answers is expressly insufficient and can make the response non-compliant.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must articulate the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must address environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must address identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must address the hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must address region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency is listed as a scope bullet without detailed acceptable locations; vendors must avoid assuming offshore/region flexibility without documenting approach.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach required, but no approved regions are specified in the RFP text.

requirements.text: Vendors must address disaster recovery, high availability, and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs, and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios, including Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid where applicable, and clearly state assumptions.
The response must cover enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.
The response must cover commercial off-the-shelf and internally developed applications.
The response must cover Windows and Linux virtual machines.
The response must cover databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL, and other supported platforms.
Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, and evidence of certifications is required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is buried in the compliance section and specific standards are not identified, so failure to attach certification evidence could make the bid non-compliant.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are required but not specifically named.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required; specific certifications are not named in the RFP text.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a DCaaS-specific mandatory requirement hidden in a note under Compliance & Regulatory Requirements and points to the Vendor Response Template for more information.
requirements.cert_required: DCaaS compliance attestations as per industry standards.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset.
Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
Vendors are expected to use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Pricing must be transparent, structured, and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.

Vendors must provide a comprehensive cost breakup including foundational setup costs such as landing zone, security, governance, and compliance configurations; application and infrastructure hosting costs; migration or implementation costs if applicable; and any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency compliance is a simple formatting issue that can make commercials non-comparable and non-compliant.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.

Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Cost protections are expected and failure to address them may weaken commercial scoring.

requirements.text: The proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Net 90 payment terms are not labeled as an automatic reject, but the RFP states anything less will not be considered competitive.

requirements.text: Vendors must ensure all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP and must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This mandatory confidentiality obligation is hidden in Appendix 8.1 rather than the main response instructions.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the solution being proposed.

The governance overview should include indicative governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework for issue resolution, and high-level key roles and responsibilities.
Selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope only in read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent scope is limited and must not be treated as broad OT migration or control-system operation.

requirements.text: Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including roles and responsibilities, governance, security and compliance approach, and operations and support model.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification process uses both a timeline and a Q&A template; missed clarification deadline can prevent assumptions from being resolved.

ambiguities: Estimated deal size is not explicitly stated in the RFP. The value provided is inferred from the enterprise data center exit / hosting evaluation scope, SAP and non-SAP workload coverage, landing zone, DR, networking, and possible managed services pricing requested.

Contract duration is not explicitly stated. The RFP says this is a Phase 0 evaluation and that detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements, SLAs, and service delivery frameworks will be finalized in later phases.
Detailed evaluation criteria, scoring structure, sub-categories, and weightages are not included in the PDF text and are stated to be in a separate Evaluation Template Excel file.
The detailed inventory baseline and counts/sizing are referenced as attached online but are not included in the provided document, limiting precise sizing and pricing analysis.
Specific compliance certifications required are not named; the document only states that evidence of certifications is required and that DCaaS providers must provide compliance attestations.
Proposal due dates list month/day but not year or time zone. Based on the document date, dates are interpreted as 2026.
The designated communication channel appears to be the contacts in Section 3.3, but the RFP does not explicitly state acceptable file transfer mechanisms or portal requirements.
hard_disqualifiers: Proposal timing/channel disqualifier: proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel. Exact text: "Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel. (Refer Section 3.3)" [PAGE 8]

Mandatory response template disqualifier: vendors are required to submit using the Vendor Response Template. Exact text: "Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions." [PAGE 9]

Incomplete response disqualifier: simple Yes/No answers are expressly insufficient. Exact text: "Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient." [PAGE 8]

Compliance certification evidence disqualifier: evidence of certifications is required. Exact text: "Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required." [PAGE 7]

DCaaS-specific hidden compliance disqualifier: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations. Exact text: "Note: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards." [PAGE 7]
Currency disqualifier: all commercial/pricing values must be quoted in USD. Exact text: "All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD)." [PAGE 9]

Appendix confidentiality/legal disqualifier: vendors must not share Kohler information with third parties without prior written consent. Exact text: "Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler." [PAGE 11]
compliance_red_flags: Hidden appendix confidentiality obligations: vendors must use Kohler information solely for responding to the RFP and must not disclose, distribute, or share information with third parties without prior written consent. [PAGE 11]
Compliance certification evidence is mandatory but specific standards are not named, creating risk of an incomplete response. [PAGE 7]
DCaaS providers have a separate mandatory requirement to explicitly provide compliance attestations, referenced to the Vendor Response Template. [PAGE 7]
Data residency approach is required in the technical scope, but no acceptable regions or sovereignty constraints are defined in the RFP text. [PAGE 6]
Selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope only in read-only or controlled integration scenarios, which creates operational/security boundary risk. [PAGE 6]
All pricing must be quoted in USD. Non-USD commercial submissions would be non-compliant. [PAGE 9]
Payment terms below net 90 are stated as not competitive, creating a significant commercial risk even though not expressly framed as automatic rejection. [PAGE 10]

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation, UK, 5 years
similar_deals.why_relevant: Closest loss analogue by manufacturing sector, multi-year transformation profile, and target future deal-size band; directly informs the current RFP's many eliminatory response and compliance requirements.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost due to a missed local-entity requirement in the compliance appendix; compliance hygiene can decide the outcome before technical scoring.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation, Netherlands, 4 years
similar_deals.why_relevant: Manufacturing, IT transformation, and within the inferred $15M-$50M downstream opportunity range; highly relevant to compliance-first bid management.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Another loss caused by a missed local-entity requirement, reinforcing that mandatory forms, appendices, assumptions, and eligibility terms must be checked before solutioning.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation, Germany, 7 years
similar_deals.why_relevant: Manufacturing transformation of similar size; supports win themes around rapid Phase 0 mobilization, named teams, and credible ramp from assessment into execution.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Fast mobilization and strong sector credentials were decisive.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout, Germany/USA/Mexico, 4 years
similar_deals.why_relevant: Larger than the likely Polaris downstream range but relevant because Project Polaris includes SAP/non-SAP hosting, global regions, manufacturing continuity, and migration risk.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Executive sponsor alignment, SAP depth, zero-downtime methodology, and global follow-the-sun delivery were decisive.

similar_deals.title: Banking IT Transformation, USA, 3 years
similar_deals.why_relevant: Not manufacturing, but relevant by US geography, enterprise IT transformation scope, and similar future deal size/duration; highlights the need for US delivery credibility and trust-building.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Domain depth, client trust, local eligibility, and prior relationship were key win factors.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation, UAE, 3 years
similar_deals.why_relevant: Larger and different geography, but supports use of manufacturing references, assessment accelerators, migration tooling, and risk-reduction IP.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Manufacturing credentials and proprietary IP reduced perceived delivery risk.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Honest assessed win probability is 43%. Positive evidence: the past-proposal layer contains highly specific Project Polaris pursuit content, including a directly aligned 12-week/three-month vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, target-state architecture coverage, SAP/VMware/DCaaS/cloud/hybrid assessment methods, open-book USD TCO, and price anchors of $595,000 for full Phase 0 and $279,533 for a lean version. The deal corpus also contains relevant manufacturing transformation wins, especially DEAL-089, DEAL-003, and DEAL-038, showing that manufacturing credentials, SAP depth, fast mobilization, global delivery, and proprietary accelerators can win similar transformation work. Negative evidence: there is no public procurement data, so competitor win rates and award pricing cannot be validated; the procurement data layer therefore cannot confirm price-to-win or competitor weakness. The RFP has multiple eliminatory requirements, and comparable manufacturing losses DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 were caused by compliance appendix failures, which is directly relevant because this RFP requires use of a specific template, detailed answers, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, and strict submission controls. Incumbent risk is also material because prior Project Polaris proposal intelligence notes Cognizant may already have cloud, data center, security, and observability responsibilities, which likely gives an incumbent advantage in data access and client trust. Confidence level: medium. The analysis uses strong past-proposal and deal-corpus evidence, but public procurement ground truth is unavailable, so the probability is deliberately kept below 50% despite good solution fit.
capability_gaps: Vendor Response Template and Evaluation Template are not yet confirmed in hand; this is traceable to the eliminatory requirement to submit using Kohler's provided template and complete it in detail, not with Yes/No answers.

Compliance certification evidence package is not yet evidenced in the supplied intelligence; this is traceable to the requirement that vendors demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards and provide evidence of certifications.

If positioning DCaaS, explicit DCaaS compliance attestations are not yet evidenced; this is traceable to the RFP note that DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.

Named US/Wisconsin delivery model, local contracting entity, and escalation coverage are not evidenced in the supplied Project Polaris content; this is traceable to the US/Wisconsin/global delivery context and deal-corpus lessons where local-entity/compliance issues caused losses in manufacturing transformation deals.

Incumbent coexistence and data-access plan must be hardened; past proposal intelligence notes Cognizant may already hold cloud, data center, security, and observability responsibilities, making access to CMDB, VMware, SAP, network, DR, and cost data a dependency for the Phase 0 deliverables.

Public procurement award data is unavailable, so competitor pricing and win-rate assumptions cannot be validated against government contract ground truth; this weakens confidence in any price-to-win view.
recommended_win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab: position Phase 0 as a 12-week/three-month decision factory that compares public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration workload by workload, rather than forcing a cloud-first answer.

Manufacturing-safe SAP and VMware data-center-exit roadmap: emphasize SAP/non-SAP dependency mapping, VMware/VxRail disposition, plant and hospitality continuity, RTO/RPO tiering, and zero-disruption migration planning, reflecting lessons from manufacturing wins DEAL-089, DEAL-003, and DEAL-038.

Open-book USD TCO and FinOps transparency: provide workload placement scorecards, licensing assumptions, run-cost baselines, migration economics, and FinOps guardrails, with all pricing in USD to satisfy the RFP commercial requirement.

Executable target-state architecture: include multi-account/multi-subscription landing-zone blueprints, environment segregation, identity integration, hybrid connectivity, region/data-residency strategy, DR/HA/scalability patterns, observability, and reference diagrams.

Compliance-first, Kohler-owned outputs: make assumptions, dependencies, constraints, certifications, optional services, confidentiality controls, and response-template completion impossible to miss; deliver outputs Kohler can use with any downstream provider.
risk_factors: No public procurement data is available, so there is no ground-truth validation of competitor awards, competitor pricing, or whether a specific rival is consistently winning this category of work.

Incumbent risk is material: prior Project Polaris proposal intelligence references Cognizant as potentially incumbent across cloud, data center, security, and observability, which may give them better current-state data, trust, and operational context.

Administrative non-compliance risk is high because the RFP contains multiple disqualifiers: deadline/channel, mandatory template, detailed answers beyond Yes/No, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, and confidentiality obligations.
Deal-corpus losses DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 show that manufacturing IT transformation pursuits can be lost despite solution fit when local-entity or appendix compliance requirements are missed.

Phase 0 scope could be confused with the much larger downstream transformation; over-selling a $15M-$50M future program inside an assessment RFP may create buyer resistance unless optional services are separated clearly.

Win evidence is good but not definitive: the past proposal intelligence is highly tailored to Project Polaris, but it is proposal content rather than confirmed award data, and there is no public procurement layer to validate market outcomes.

Kohler may prefer a provider with existing operational accountability or DCaaS assets if the evaluation is weighted toward continuity, current-state knowledge, or owned facilities rather than vendor-neutral advisory.
must_fix_before_bidding: Obtain and use Kohler's Vendor Response Template and any missing Evaluation Template/Excel scoring model before drafting final response.

Build a line-by-line compliance matrix covering submission deadline/channel, detailed template completion, USD pricing, assumptions/dependencies, optional services separation, confidentiality restrictions, certification evidence, and DCaaS attestations.
Prepare a complete certification and compliance evidence pack, including cloud hosting, security, regional/industry standards, and DCaaS attestations if DCaaS is included.
Separate the committed Phase 0 assessment scope from optional downstream migration, hosting, DCaaS, landing-zone buildout, DR implementation, FinOps, AIOps, and managed services.

Use a fixed-fee Phase 0 price anchored in prior Project Polaris proposal intelligence, with $595,000 as the best-fit full-scope option and a clearly scoped lean alternative only if Kohler wants reduced scope.
Name the Phase 0 leadership team, mobilization plan, week-by-week workshop calendar, SME dependencies, deliverables, and governance model.

Explicitly address incumbent-provider coexistence, data access, shared-responsibility RACI, and transition boundaries, especially where current operations, observability, security, and infrastructure data may sit with another provider.

client_intel.recommended_narrative: Position Polaris as a secure hybrid operating model, not a simple hosting choice. Emphasize workload placement, AIOps observability, cyber resilience, data governance, cost transparency and clean integration with Kohler’s existing Cognizant-led ecosystem.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: high, confidence medium-high — Evidence supports preparation: a recent Waukesha, Wisconsin TCS listing referenced Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, AWS, Python and Java, and TCS has a very recent US$800M SKF global manufacturing/infrastructure/app/data/connectivity managed-services win. No recent contract wins in Wisconsin were found. Sources: Indeed-indexed Waukesha listing; TCS May/July 2026 SKF and Q1 FY27 releases.
competitors.predicted_positioning: TCS will position as the safest global manufacturing transformation partner: 'board-room-to-shop-floor' manufacturing expertise, AI-led transformation, hybrid cloud, landing zones, SAP HANA on cloud, BYOL, colocation, zero trust, resiliency, and end-to-end managed services. Their proposal angle will likely connect Kohler's data-center exit and VMware/SAP/non-SAP estate to a larger AI-enabled global operations modernization. Sources: TCS Enterprise Cloud pages; TCS manufacturing pages; TCS SKF US$800M announcement; TCS Q1 FY27 order book US$9.5B. Confidence: medium-high.
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: Phase 0 assessment likely $450k-$900k; full 3-5 year hosting/transformation pursuit likely $25M-$50M if they can attach managed services. Calculation: no public procurement awards are available from Source 2. TCS Q1 FY27 revenue was US$7.624B and operating income US$1.826B, operating margin about 24.0%; pricing floor multiplier = 1/(1-0.24)=1.32x cost. Revenue/employee cannot be calculated from provided sources because current TCS headcount was not provided. A $450k-$900k Phase 0 bid implies an internal delivery-cost band of about $342k-$684k at a 24% margin. Confidence: medium because price is margin-grounded but not public-contract-grounded.
their_strengths: Very recent US$800M SKF global AI-led manufacturing transformation including infrastructure, applications, data, security, end-user services and connectivity. Source: TCS SKF announcement and Q1 FY27 release.
Strong profitability gives delivery capacity: Q1 FY27 revenue US$7.624B, operating income US$1.826B, operating margin about 24.0%, net margin 19.2%. Source: TCS Q1 FY27 results.

Hybrid cloud offering directly maps to RFP requirements: landing-zone architecture, SAP HANA on cloud, IaaS/PaaS, hybrid orchestration, BYOL, colocation, zero trust and resiliency. Source: TCS Enterprise Cloud materials.
Manufacturing is a defined TCS vertical, 8.7% of Q1 FY27 revenue, with stated support for 100+ manufacturers globally. Source: TCS Q1 FY27 and manufacturing pages.
their_weaknesses: No recent Wisconsin-specific TCS cloud/DCaaS/digital innovation contract win was found; only a point-in-time Waukesha job listing surfaced. Source: web/job search results.
Margin is high: a 24.0% operating margin creates less room to underprice without sacrificing profitability; pricing floor multiplier is 1.32x delivery cost. Source: TCS Q1 FY27 financials.

Large-deal momentum may bias them toward a broad managed-services upsell rather than a strictly neutral Phase 0 hosting-options evaluation. Source: TCS SKF US$800M deal and US$9.5B Q1 FY27 order book.

Public procurement price anchors are unavailable, so Kohler should demand transparent unit rates, assumptions, offshore/onshore mix and pass-through cloud/DCaaS pricing. Source: Source 2 no public procurement data available.
how_to_beat_them: Force the contest into an unbiased Phase 0 decision model rather than an outsourcing-scale managed-services sales cycle: TCS will likely try to use its SKF US$800M global manufacturing transformation reference to pull Kohler toward a broad infrastructure/app/data/security/connectivity managed-services model, so require a Kohler-owned options scorecard, workload-by-workload placement rationale, and transparent cloud/DCaaS/colocation economics before any run-services award. Source: TCS Q1 FY27 release and SKF announcement.

Exploit the Wisconsin evidence gap: TCS has only a point-in-time Waukesha, WI software-developer listing with OCI/AWS/Python/Java and no public Wisconsin-specific cloud/DCaaS deal found. Require named Midwest/Wisconsin delivery leads, onsite discovery commitments, and comparable U.S. manufacturing/DC-exit references. Source: Indeed-indexed Waukesha listing; TCS careers/news search.

Attack compliance and template execution: Kohler has disqualifiers for certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, template completeness, and confidentiality. Make our response a compliance-first submission with attached SOC/ISO/CSA/PCI-style evidence matrix, assumptions register, and line-by-line RFP template completion; TCS scale can create generic responses if not tightly localized. Source: RFP pages 7-11.

Price below a TCS premium assessment but not irrationally: TCS Q1 FY27 operating margin is about 24.0%, implying a sustainable price floor multiplier of 1/(1-0.24)=1.32x delivery cost; a fixed Phase 0 price in the $325k-$575k band pressures a likely TCS $450k-$900k assessment without looking under-scoped. Source: TCS Q1 FY27 financials; Source 2 states no public procurement data available.

pursuit_plan.strategy: Pursue this as a strategic Phase 0 entry point to shape Kohler’s future data center exit and hybrid hosting transformation. Lead with an unbiased, evidence-based comparison of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options, supported by strong compliance evidence, architecture diagrams, migration risk controls, and transparent TCO. Avoid a generic cloud migration pitch; Kohler needs confidence that manufacturing continuity, SAP/database complexity, DR, and global governance will be protected.
pursuit_plan.win_strategy: Win by being the safest and most commercially transparent advisor for Kohler’s hosting decision: show a rigorous, inventory-driven, compliance-backed comparison of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options that reduces data center exit risk while positioning a clear execution path for the larger transformation.
pursuit_plan.pricing_approach: Use a hybrid competitive/value-based pricing strategy. Price Phase 0 as a transparent fixed-fee assessment in USD, with clearly defined deliverables, workshops, inventory analysis, target-state options, migration roadmap, risk assessment, and 3-5 year TCO comparison; keep it competitive enough to win a land-and-expand opportunity. For future hosting/transformation scenarios, provide indicative range-based TCO models for Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid, separating one-time migration, recurring platform, managed services, licensing, network, DR, tooling, and optional services; include assumptions and sensitivity bands because inventory and evaluation weights are incomplete.
key_risks: The RFP is Phase 0 assessment-only, but the strategic value depends on shaping later hosting/migration/managed services phases; the proposal must win trust without overcommitting future scope.

Seven eliminatory requirements create high compliance risk: wrong submission channel, missed template, insufficient detail, missing certification evidence, missing DCaaS attestations, non-USD pricing, or mishandled confidentiality could disqualify the bid.

Evaluation weightings are not provided, so the response must be balanced across architecture, migration, networking, DR, operating model, tools, and cost transparency rather than over-optimizing for one dimension.
Inventory data is referenced but not included in the decomposition; inaccurate sizing, pricing, or migration assumptions could undermine credibility.
Compliance certifications are not named, creating risk that required attestations vary by workload, geography, industry standard, or DCaaS provider.
Kohler’s SAP HANA, Oracle, SQL Server, VMware, and DR requirements may create licensing and cost traps, especially given VMware/Broadcom commercial changes and database license mobility constraints.

Hybrid connectivity and global data residency requirements may require network carriers, cloud providers, or DCaaS partners; third-party data sharing must be controlled under the confidentiality clause.
Competitors may bid Phase 0 aggressively as a loss leader to capture the larger transformation program, creating price pressure.
A biased cloud-only or DCaaS-only recommendation could score poorly because Kohler is explicitly asking for evaluation of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options.
Future legal terms, SLAs, delivery model, and compliance obligations are deferred to later phases, so assumptions and exclusions must be explicit to avoid downstream commercial exposure.
